speaker: interviewer
text: Max Eisen. Please continue with your story, Max.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here we are on this train. And as I mentioned before, Jewish communities were bringing food at every stop. And whenever the train stopped, they helped us with food and so on. And we could notice that there was an indecision about this whole transport, because we went ahead to the next town, which was called Rachov. This was in the Carpathian. And then we were standing there for about a day. The train was shifted back. We came back to Satmar. And we were standing there. And then, again, we were shipped forth again. So we were being shunted back and forth for several days.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember, the train was stopped in Rachov. And there was a troop-- a hospital troop train, a Hungarian troop train, coming from the Russian front with wounded, you know? We were standing side by side. And some of these soldiers, when they saw us, you know, they could tell, you know, that we were Jews. And I remember one soldier was yelling, 'You stinking Jews. You'll be floating in the Dniester--' which is a river, '--like leaves from a tree.' You know, this is in Hungarian. You'll-- [HUNGARIAN] You know? So, at first, I couldn't understand what he was talking about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, the train went on. And we arrived at a place called, [NON-ENGLISH], which was near the [NON-ENGLISH], which is the pass over the Carpathian Mountains to go to Ukraine, I guess. And we arrived in this big sawmill. And we were handled by Hungarian MPs. There were the gendarme. They are front line gendarmes. And we had to line up. And they were checking all our luggage. And there were two brothers from my town. Their name was Bodner. One was Bodner Yidu. I forget the name of the other one. And they were checking their suitcase.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember, this gendarme took out a bottle of jam that they had. And he took his bayonet out, and he put this bayonet into the jar of jam. And he was mixing it around. And all of a sudden, he pulled out a watch with a gold chain on it, you know. And I was absolutely amazed to see something like that. To me, that was-- I didn't understand this, you know? How does a watch get into a jar of jam?

speaker: interviewee
text: And right away, they started to tear their clothes apart. And I remember they tore their jackets apart. And they found money in their shoulder pads. And, you know, to me, this was such a new thing that I've never seen before. And I was 13 years old at this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here we were. We were put into a-- it was not a barrack, but it was a big room, I guess, where there was some equipment, where they were cutting wood and stuff like that. And what I noticed that all around on the walls on these wooden planks, there were hundreds and hundreds of names of Jewish families.

speaker: interviewee
text: It said, for instance, just to say a name, say Berkowitz family from Budapest. On this day, we were taken to [NON-ENGLISH]. And what I remember, that all of these families were taken to [NON-ENGLISH], I had no idea where this [NON-ENGLISH] was. And we were there for approximately two weeks. There was a kitchen set up by the Hungarian Jewish community. And we were given a bowl of soup a day. But pretty well, we had to buy-- the money that we brought with us, I remember, we had to buy some of these small, little, black rye breads from the native Ruthenian people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They sold us these little breads for a lot of money. I can't recall exactly what we paid for it, but I know it was an awful lot of money. And as long as my mother had some money, we were able to eat bread. And we had a bowl of soup. And that's about it, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day, the gendarmes used to take us to the stream of the Tisza River, where it starts from the Carpathian Mountains, to get some drinking water to bring back to this lumber yard. So about a couple of weeks later, we were lined up one morning. And we were told that, this afternoon, we're being transported to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I think we were approximately about 400 people, as best as I can recollect.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know some of the other places they were from, besides Moldova?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know there were some people from a place called, Mateszalka, which was also in Hungary, and some from Kasha. It was a mixed group, because, from our town, from my town, I think there were only about four families taken away at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this officer told us that morning that we're going to be taking on a transport to [NON-ENGLISH]. And the afternoon came around, and the trucks came. And we were loaded up on these trucks. And the trucks were ready to roll.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't see this officer. He was a Hungarian officer, a rank of captain, I believe he was. I remember, he had a big mustache. And he was a very nice man. In fact, he didn't give us any grief.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was an army person. We had more problem with gendarmes, you know? They were very brutal. So all of a sudden, this Hungarian officer came galloping on his horse. And he yelled, and he said, stop the transport. And everybody down from the trucks. And he told us that we are going home. And, you know, we were really pleased to hear this, you know, because that was unexpected, altogether, you know? We didn't expect this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to us, look, there's a train waiting down at the station. And you'll have to buy your fare back home. And those people that don't have money, the others will help them to buy their tickets home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we've got our few rags together, and we marched down to the train station. The station was called, [NON-ENGLISH]. We got on this train, and we started our trek back home. And this was a trip, I guess, about two, three days, because it was a slow train. It was not an express, it was a train that stopped at every stop. How were the people faring, condition-wise? Was there adequate food and facilities in the sawmill?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the sawmill, there was-- the only food we had was one bowl of soup a day that I mentioned to you, that the Hungarian-Jewish community set up a kitchen. And we were served one bowl of soup a day. And the rest-- that's all we had. We had to buy this little black bread from the native people. That's all we had. We have nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated by the authorities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were-- we had no-- we were treated all right. I don't remember any beatings or anything of that sort. I remember one thing, though, that when we arrived, as we were being checked through by these gendarmes, the military police, I remember some other guards came. And about 100 feet away from where I was standing, I could see there was something on the ground, like a metal grate, or something like.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these gendarmes went and they picked up these metal grates. And they started to yell. And all of a sudden, soldiers were coming out of there wearing Russian uniform. You know, they had a certain type of a cap, you know? And I was so shocked to see that people are living in a hole in the ground. And they were beaten, you know? And they were formed up into groups and marched off somewhere. I don't know where. These were prisoners that were brought back from the Russian front. And this is the way they were kept in this place. But, you know, we were treated-- I wasn't beaten or anything like that, you know? Food was very scarce. So the mood was not uneasy?

speaker: interviewee
text: The which?

speaker: interviewer
text: The mood of the group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was not uneasy, you know. We sort of-- I don't know-- we sort of managed to roll with the punches. When I look back, I'm amazed, you know, but, at that time, we were not used to all these luxuries, you know? We were able to live-- we lived in a very modest way, you know? And I guess we just got used to living that way. And you did the best you could.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here I am on this train back. And we got off in a place called, Cop. We had some relatives living there. I know this, that we were very filthy, because there was no way we could wash ourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had these relatives living in Cop. So my mother said, let's get off the train. So it was my mother, my aunt, myself, and my two little brothers. And we came into our relatives. And I remember we had a good wash. And we washed our clothes and everything else. And we had a good meal. And we stayed over for one night. I remember, slept in a bed, you know, for a change, not on a floor board, you know, on sawdust. And that was very good. And the next day, we got on a train, and we started our journey back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I just want to tell you. When we were taken away, I didn't mention to you that my grandfather and my grandmother were not taken away with us. This was really odd, you know. My grandfather, because he was a war veteran, I guess, maybe that was the reason they didn't take him away. But I remember he ran to the train that morning, when they were taking us to the train, with some milk for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he went back home, he sent a telegram to the labor camp where my father and uncle were and told them that we were taken away. And my father and uncle, they asked for leave from the officers to let them go home and see-- to look after this situation. And they were not allowed to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember, several days after, they escaped from this labor camp. And they came back home to our town. And they got the story of what happened. And what they did is they hired a taxi, and they tracked-- they tried to find out where this train was going. And it took them quite a while, until they got a thread of a lead, you know, where we were taken. And they sort of followed all the way up. And they came up to-- to this place in [NON-ENGLISH], a couple of days after we have left for home, you see? So then, they started to follow the train back. And we got off the train, so they missed us. They caught up to the train at another station, and we were not on it. So anyway, we got home before they did. And I remember coming back to my little town. And this was-- it was in July, I guess. And I had a lot of big story to tell all my friends, you know. I remember, my grandmother, she cooked up a big-- she made a chicken, you know? And I just remember the smell. And the cucumber salad, it's still with me. And then, all of us kids, we went to the river, you know, to swim and so on. And I was a real big hero. I could tell a big story, you know? None of us kids have traveled on such a long journey ever before, you know, in this little town. So my father came home-- and my uncle. And I don't know. I just can't remember how they settled this situation for them deserting, you know, the labor camp. But I think it was settled in some way. So this was 1942. And by this time, I was out of school, you know, because there was no point in me going to school. And the Hebrew school was finished, because the teacher was taken away to forced labor camp as well. And I can't say that I was too sad about that, because there was no Hebrew school. I was kind of happy about that, you know? So life went on. And here comes 1943. My father and uncle came back from the forced labor camp. And I think they found a way of handling things, that they found a job in a lumber mill about 30, 40 kilometers from my town. And both of them were working there as essential labor, so they will not be taken away to forced labor camp. And I know that they came home-- this was all of 1943, approximately-- they came home every Friday afternoon for the Sabbath. And my mother had breads baked for them and some other stuff, you know? And after the Sabbath, Sunday morning, early morning, they packed their backpacks and they went to the train station. And they were off for a whole week again to do this labor. And this is the way they kept themselves out of the labor camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in 1943, as well, I left home. I went to this town of Kasha. And I became an apprentice at a furrier place. I lived with some relatives. And I lived there and I ate with them. And I had to get up every morning, about 5:30 in the morning, to get down to this place of business where the furrier was. And, you know, as an apprentice, I had to clean the stove, take the clinkers and the ashes out every morning, and sweep the floors, and put everything in order, and start the fire. And I was given the so-called dirty jobs. The furs, after they are repaired-- or before-- they have to be cleaned.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember, I had to put these coats on a table. And there was a chemical mixed with sawdust that I had to pour this on the fur and sort of rub it, you know, really hard for quite a while, until the shine came up on the fur, you know? And I remember, it really was hard on my hands, because I didn't have gloves to wear. And it must have been some kind of a chemical that was mixed in with the sawdust. So, for me, this was a new venture, you know? You can understand, coming from a little town, all of a sudden, I'm in a big city. In this city, there were about 6,000 Jewish people living. And it was a city, probably, about 20,000 to 25,000 people. There were street cars. There was an opera house. And so this was quite a large city.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had to deliver fur coats. I remember, I had two handfuls of coats with addresses, you know, where I had to deliver and get on the street car with these fur coats. And my only pay was the tips that I was getting when I delivered these coats.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, again, every Friday afternoon, I used to get on the train go home for the Sabbath until Sunday morning. And I was quite a big shot, you know? I was 13, 14, and I had money in my pocket, you know? And I was telling the other kids how life is in this big city, you see? So this went on right up until 1944, early 1944, I guess. And in March-- March of 1944, the Germans occupied Hungary. And I remember this transport, this troop-- troop of trucks. In the middle of the night, we could hear trucks going by, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we looked out-- we had to have-- the windows had to be covered with black cloth, you know, because these were the laws, that we had to cover this, so that there's no light comes through the-- the window. But we could hear these trucks going by.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the morning, we could see there was a whole bunch of trucks parked in town, you know? And these were German trucks with the swastikas, with a cross on it. And this was the first time I saw Germans. And it was a frightening thing, you know? I've never seen soldiers like that in my life. I remember the shivers going through.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they sort of kept to themselves. And in a while, they were gone. And came Passover of 1944, and, you know, we had a-- the Passover meal was always-- this was a big-- the Seder was a big affair, you know. It was in my grandfather's quarters. Each of us had separate kitchens, ourselves, my grandparents, and my uncle and aunt. But for the Seder, you know, for the Passover meal, we were all together in my grandfather's quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember there was a big, long table set up with all these goodies, you know, with all the foods and things. And the whole family was there. And this was the last time that our family was sitting together at a-- at a table together like that. And my younger brother, you know, as is customary, asked the questions, you know-- why is this night different from any other night? And we ate the customary foods, you know? Who ran the Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran the Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather ran the Seder. He was the head of the family. And he was the paternal figure, you know, in the house. And when the Seder was finished, which was quite late, I would say, after 11 o'clock at night, I remember my grandfather, my father, my uncle, and myself, we went out in the yard. It was probably-- well, it was in April. And it was quite a balmy night. It was very nice, pleasant outside. And I remember hearing planes flying overhead, a lot of planes. You know, this happened, you know, several times before. And we realized that these were planes going somewhere. We didn't know actually where they were going. But I found out after, you know, that these were American planes going on bombing flights to Poland and many other different places. And it was a beautiful moonlit night. And, you know, this was '44, in April, you know. We figured that we are going to be OK, that we'll sort of ride out the war. And we'll be at home. And everything will be fine. Because we were sort of aware of what was happening on the Russian front. You know, you sort of had to read between the lines in the newspapers, because you have to know that the newspapers, everything, was censored. You know, they would not put in anything that would give you any clue.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I remember, we had a map. And they usually-- the way they wrote it, they said that the German troops have pulled back to previously prepared positions. So then, we understood. You know, even I, as a child, you know, we already knew this, that this meant that they are running, you know? It was not a previously prepared position, but they were really getting the heck knocked out of them, you know? So we thought that the Russians will be liberating us in no time at all, you know? But of course, that's not what happened. Because, that night, we went to bed-- it must have been about 1 o'clock.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we had our compound, you know, around the lumber yard and our garden, we had a very tall fence with barbed wire, because we wanted to keep people out at night from stealing lumber or coming to steal fruit. And we had a-- I had a big dog, a German shepherd. And we had several fox terriers. And this German shepherd was running on a wire the full length of our yard and the garden as well. And the dogs were really barking. And my father looked through-- looked out the window. And I woke up. And he asked, who is there. And I could hear him talk. And he got out of bed, and he went to open the gates to our yard. You know, we had big wooden gates. And this fellow came in with this horse and buggy. And my father took him in to grandfather's quarters. This fellow was a forester. I've known him. And I remember he was telling my grandfather-- he said, Mr. Eisen, I came to take your family away, because, tomorrow morning, all the Jews will be taken away from this town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know, my grandfather says, well, it's a holiday. This is [YIDDISH], you know? It's Passover, you know? We're not allowed-- we're not permitted to-- you know? So we just stayed, you know? And this man was really upset. He couldn't understand this. But-- Did they have a long--

speaker: interviewee
text: --what grandfather said, you know, this was the last word, you know? And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a long discussion over this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? It was quite a discussion. And he left, you know? And we just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name? The forester?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember his name. I remember he had a green uniform on that he always used to wear, you know, like britches, you know, with high boots. And he had this hunting hat, you know, with-- not feathers, but like a bristle, you know, from-- And he-- well, I guess he must have-- wherever he was the night before, you know, in a pub or somewhere, I guess, he heard this from the gendarmes, when they were drinking there. Somebody must have slipped a word, you know, that this is what's going to happen the next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he left. The gates were locked. And I guess we went back to bed. There wasn't much sleep after that, because, at the crack of dawn-- it was probably 6:30, I guess, in the morning-- an unusual thing happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we had a town crier in town, because that's the way the news were reported, you know, any laws from the mayor, or whatever. There was this little fellow who went to different spots in town. And he had a drum. And he played his drum for a few minutes. And that meant that everybody has to come and listen what he has to say. And then he took out this roll of paper. And he had a very loud voice. And he felt he was a very important person. And this happened right in front of our house. And, you know, we belonged to a different municipality. And we never had this town drummer in front of our house. And this happened that morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, you know, we looked out, and he pulls out this piece of paper. And he's reading out that all Jews must stay in their homes. They're not to leave their house. So that was very unusual, you know? We didn't know what this meant.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a little while later, gendarmes came in. And they came into our house. And they said, you have a few minutes to pack a bundle-- and it was only to be five kilograms, or something like that-- and we are taking you away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to me, this was like-- you know, like something-- like a reel that is playing again, you know? I've gone through this once before. And there was real pandemonium, you know? My father was yelling at me to put your boots on. And my mother had the baby. My little sister was nine months old. And you can imagine what you-- what do you do in a situation like this? I remember, we had some pillow cases. We threw some stuff in it, because, you know, we didn't have suitcases, you know, stuff like that. Not because we were that poor, but, you know, we didn't need any. You know, we were not traveling on trips, or something like that. So we just grabbed whatever we could, whatever food there was. Did you have valuables too?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had valuables. And I remember-- oh, I remember, at this time, we had a neighbor. She lived across the street from us. She was-- her name was, Ili Klinka. She was a Christian woman. And she was a very good friend of my mother and my aunt. And, in fact, I went to school with her son. His name was, Nori, N-O-R-I, Klinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she came running into the house when the gendarmes came in. And she was telling my mother-- she says, Ethel, what are you doing with this baby. Where are you taking this child, you know? Leave the child with me, you know. And she was begging, you know, to leave this baby with her. And, of course, my mother would not leave the baby, you know? And at that time, my father-- I remember this-- that he was trying to hand her something in a little cotton bag, you know? And the gendarmes caught him, you know. They caught his hand. And they took away this bag that was-- and I remember, the gendarme sort of poured it out on his hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember these gold-- gold coins. They were called Maria Theresas, you know. They were gold ducats, you know. There were some-- and some rings, and stuff like that. He wanted to leave it with her, I suppose. But it was taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so it was absolutely chaos, you know? And we were marched out from the house. And we left everything behind, you know, our furniture, linen, and, you know, everything, you know. And I was very upset about the dogs, that I have to leave my dogs, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we were taken out of the house, all these neighbors were standing around, you know. And nobody really had anything good to say. And in fact, I found out that, after we went around the corner, you know, all these neighbors, they descended on our home, like in every other Jewish home where the people were taken out. And they looted everything that could be moved, you know. And, eventually, they moved in. And in fact, that's the only way-- this friend of ours, Ili, you know, she managed to save a few pictures that I had. That's the only way I-- she saved those pictures. And when I came back, she handed me those few pictures of my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were taken through the town like a bunch of criminals into the schoolhouse. And this was the school where I went. We were put into the school rooms. And all the people were collected from this town and neighboring little villages and farms. And this took a couple of days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, life changed instantly, you know. Once the first night we had to spend in the schoolhouse on the floor--

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody there was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was Jewish, yeah. These were only Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did life change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, you know, you were sitting on a bench, you were sleeping on the floor. There was no regular meals anymore. And everything was just irregular, you know? It was a total change.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were there with your grandparents and the rest of the town, you know. And, you know, there was no more privacy. This was the first time that we were all sort of thrown together in one room, everybody from town, you know? And children, the babies were screaming. And, you know, there was no meals being cooked. You know, you just ate what you brought with you, you know? And I remember this was Passover, you know? And that we had no bread. We just had the matzah, you know? And that can't-- you know, you are pretty hungry if you're eating that kind of stuff, you know? So, you know, we had no baked bread, you see? So after two days, we were taken through the town and taken to the railroad yard.
